sentences.base_language: One morning, Antoine woke up with a headache and a stomachache, and felt very tired.
sentences.target_language: Un matin, Antoine s'est réveillé avec un mal de tête et des maux d'estomac, et il se sentait très fatigué.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Mal de tête** (headache) / **maux d'estomac** (stomach pains): **mal** (pain — singular) vs **maux** (pains — irregular plural of mal). Note the pattern: **mal de** + body part: **mal de tête** (headache), **mal de dos** (backache), **mal de gorge** (sore throat). The plural **maux** appears in set expressions: **maux d'estomac**, **maux de ventre**.
**S'est réveillé** — passé composé with **être**: the passé composé of pronominal verbs always uses **être** as auxiliary: je **me** suis réveillé, il **s'**est réveillé. The past participle agrees with the subject: elle s'est réveill**ée**. Compare: **se sentait** (imperfect — a continuous background state of tiredness, not a single event), while **s'est réveillé** (passé composé — the discrete act of waking up).

sentences.base_language: Lately he had been working a lot and wasn't sleeping well.
sentences.target_language: Dernièrement, il avait beaucoup travaillé et ne dormait pas bien.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avait travaillé** — plus-que-parfait: **avait** (imparfait de avoir) + participe passé. The plus-que-parfait expresses an action completed before another past reference point: il **avait travaillé** (he had worked — completed before the morning described). Compare: **avait été en train de travailler** (had been working — progressive, more cumbersome; French prefers the simple plus-que-parfait for this meaning).
**Ne dormait pas** — imparfait for habitual/ongoing past: he wasn't sleeping well (as an ongoing pattern). The imparfait here describes a background state — not a single night but a sustained habit. **Dernièrement** = lately/recently — a formal synonym of **récemment**. Note that **dernièrement** can only refer to the recent past (cannot refer to the future, unlike **récemment** which sometimes can).

sentences.base_language: He decided to go to the hospital near his home.
sentences.target_language: Il a décidé d'aller à l'hôpital qui se trouvait près de chez lui.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Décider de + infinitif** = to decide to do: décidé **d'**aller (decided to go). The preposition **de** links **décider** to the following infinitive. Compare: **décider que** + indicatif (decide that ~: Il a décidé **qu'**il partirait — he decided that he would leave). **Décider de** + inf. focuses on the resolution to act.
**Se trouver** = to be located, to be found: l'hôpital qui **se trouvait** (the hospital that was located). **Se trouver** is a more polished/precise alternative to **être** for expressing location: Où **se trouve** la pharmacie? (Where is the pharmacy?). The relative clause uses the **imparfait** (**se trouvait**) because the hospital's location is a background, descriptive fact — not an event.

sentences.base_language: He washed his face and got dressed, but felt too weak to eat breakfast.
sentences.target_language: Il s'est lavé le visage et s'est habillé, mais il se sentait trop faible pour prendre son petit-déjeuner.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se laver le visage** — reflexive + definite article for body parts: **le** visage (not *son* visage). In French, body parts after reflexive verbs take the definite article, not the possessive: je **me** lave **les** mains (I wash my hands), il **s'**est lavé **le** visage (he washed his face). The reflexive pronoun already indicates whose body part it is.
**Trop + adjectif + pour + infinitif** = too ~ to do: trop **faible** pour **prendre** son petit-déjeuner (too weak to eat breakfast). This mirrors the English structure exactly. Compare: **assez + adj + pour + inf** (enough to ~): assez **fort** pour **porter** ça (strong enough to carry that). The preposition **pour** before the infinitive is essential in both patterns.

sentences.base_language: The hospital was two subway stops from his apartment.
sentences.target_language: L'hôpital se trouvait à deux stations de métro de son appartement.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Être/Se trouver à + distance** = to be (a distance) away: **à deux stations** de métro (two metro stops away). The preposition **à** expresses measured distance: **à cinq minutes** à pied (five minutes on foot), **à trois kilomètres** d'ici (three kilometers from here). This **à** of distance is one of the most common preposition uses in French.
**Station de métro** vs **arrêt de bus**: **station** is used for métro and train stops; **arrêt** (stop) is used for buses and trams: **arrêt** de bus (bus stop), **station** de métro (metro station). This distinction is important in urban contexts. **De son appartement** — note **de** (from) for the starting point of a distance, mirroring **à** (at/to) for the location.

sentences.base_language: At the reception, a nurse asked him: "How can I help you?"
sentences.target_language: À l'accueil, une infirmière lui a demandé : « En quoi puis-je vous aider ? »
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lui a demandé** — indirect object **lui** (to him): **demander** takes an indirect object for the person asked: je **lui** ai demandé (I asked him/her). Compare: **lui** as indirect object (demander **à** quelqu'un) vs **le/la** as direct object (regarder quelqu'un). **Demander** always uses an indirect object for the person; the question or thing asked is the direct object: il **lui** a demandé **son** nom (he asked him his name).
**Puis-je** — formal inverted question form of **je peux**: **puis-je** is the literary/formal first-person inversion of pouvoir. In everyday speech: **est-ce que je peux vous aider?** or simply **je peux vous aider?**. **Puis** is the archaic subjunctive/inverted form of pouvoir used specifically in formal inversions: **Puis-je** entrer? (May I come in?). **En quoi** = in what way, how — a formal alternative to **comment**.

sentences.base_language: He replied: "My head and stomach hurt, and I feel completely exhausted."
sentences.target_language: Il a répondu : « J'ai mal à la tête et à l'estomac, et je me sens complètement épuisé. »
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avoir mal à + article + body part** = to have pain in ~: **j'ai mal à la tête** (my head hurts), **j'ai mal à l'estomac** (my stomach hurts). The article contracts when needed: **à la** tête, **à l'**estomac, **au** dos. This is the standard French way to express bodily pain — **ne pas confondre** with **faire mal** (to hurt someone) or **se faire mal** (to hurt oneself).
**Épuisé** = exhausted — past participle of **épuiser** (to exhaust/drain), used as adjective. **Complètement épuisé** (completely exhausted) — a very natural and common expression. Compare: **fatigué** (tired — milder), **épuisé** (exhausted — more severe, implies total depletion), **crevé** (wiped out — very colloquial). **Se sentir** + adjective: **je me sens** épuisé (I feel exhausted — describes current state).

sentences.base_language: A doctor examined him and asked: "Do you exercise regularly? How is your diet?"
sentences.target_language: Un médecin l'a examiné et lui a demandé : « Faites-vous de l'exercice régulièrement ? Comment est votre alimentation ? »
sentences.grammarNote:
**L'a examiné** — direct object pronoun **le** (him) → **l'** before vowel: the doctor examined **him** directly → **l'**. Contrast with indirect object **lui** (to him): **lui** a demandé (asked **to** him). A core French distinction: direct objects (the thing/person directly affected) use **le/la/les**; indirect objects (the recipient, with an implied **à**) use **lui/leur**.
**Faire de l'exercice** = to exercise: **faire de + partitive + noun** is the standard French way to express habitual or undetermined activities: faire **de la** natation (swim), faire **du** vélo (cycle), faire **de l'**exercice (exercise). Note that after **ne…pas** the partitive becomes **de**: il ne fait **pas d'**exercice. **Alimentation** = diet/nutrition (formal); compare **régime** (specific diet plan), **nourriture** (food in general).

sentences.base_language: He admitted he had been eating a lot of junk food and barely resting.
sentences.target_language: Il a admis qu'il mangeait beaucoup de malbouffe et qu'il se reposait très peu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Admettre que + indicatif** — reporting a fact: **admis qu'il mangeait** (admitted that he was eating). After verbs of declaring/admitting, French uses the indicatif (not subjunctif), because the content is presented as true: il **admet qu'il** a tort (admits he is wrong). The imparfait **mangeait** in the subordinate clause reflects the ongoing, habitual nature of the eating (backshift from the moment of admission).
**Malbouffe** (f.) = junk food — a French coinage: **mal** (bad) + **bouffe** (food, colloquial). **Bouffe** is informal for food (from **bouffer**, to eat — colloquial). The compound **malbouffe** is now standard in French media and conversation: **manger de la malbouffe** (to eat junk food). Compare: **nourriture malsaine** (unhealthy food — more formal). **Se reposer très peu** = to rest very little — **peu** after a verb functions as an adverb of quantity.

sentences.base_language: The doctor said: "You need more rest and a better diet. Drink plenty of water."
sentences.target_language: Le médecin a dit : « Vous avez besoin de plus de repos et d'une meilleure alimentation. Buvez beaucoup d'eau. »
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avoir besoin de + nom/infinitif** = to need: vous avez **besoin de** plus de repos (you need more rest). After **avoir besoin de**, nouns take no article (or partitive becomes **de**): besoin **de** repos (need rest — not *du* repos). Compare: **il faut + infinitif** (one must/it is necessary to — impersonal): **il faut** boire beaucoup d'eau.
**Buvez** — impératif of **boire** (vous form): the vous imperative is identical to the present indicative vous form (drop **vous**): vous buvez → **buvez**. Similarly: vous mangez → **mangez**, vous prenez → **prenez**. Exception pattern: **être** → **soyez**, **avoir** → **ayez**, **savoir** → **sachez**. The imperative of **boire** is irregular: **bois** (tu), **buvons** (nous), **buvez** (vous).

sentences.base_language: He felt a little embarrassed, but also relieved to know it wasn't serious.
sentences.target_language: Il s'est senti un peu gêné, mais aussi soulagé d'apprendre que ce n'était pas grave.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se sentir** — agreement with être auxiliary: **il s'est senti** (he felt). Pronominal verbs use **être** in the passé composé, and the past participle agrees with the subject: **elle** s'est senti**e** gêné**e** (she felt embarrassed). However, when **se sentir** is followed by an attribute, the participle agrees with the subject: **il** s'est senti **gêné** (attribute gêné, no agreement needed for the participle senti when the se is indirect — this is a subtlety).
**Soulagé d'apprendre** = relieved to learn: **soulagé de + infinitif** (relieved to ~). Many adjectives link to an infinitive with **de**: **content de** venir (happy to come), **heureux d'**apprendre (happy to learn), **soulagé de** savoir (relieved to know). Compare: **pour** + infinitif (in order to — purpose): je suis venu **pour** apprendre (I came in order to learn).

sentences.base_language: He left the hospital and found a grocery store nearby.
sentences.target_language: Il a quitté l'hôpital et a trouvé un supermarché tout près.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Quitter** vs **partir**: **quitter** (to leave — transitive, takes a direct object): **quitter** l'hôpital (leave the hospital), **quitter** quelqu'un (leave someone). **Partir** (to leave/depart — intransitive, no direct object): il est parti (he left). You cannot say *quitter de* or *partir l'hôpital* — each verb has its fixed pattern. **Partir de** = to leave from: **partir de** Paris (leave from Paris — with a location starting point).
**Tout près** = very nearby: **tout** before an adjective or adverb intensifies it: **tout** près (very close), **tout** seul (all alone), **tout** de suite (right away). This use of **tout** as an intensifier is very common in French and more natural than **très** in these fixed expressions: **tout** à fait (absolutely), **tout** à coup (suddenly), **tout** à l'heure (in a moment/just now).

sentences.base_language: He bought vegetables, fish, and fruit — no snacks this time.
sentences.target_language: Il a acheté des légumes, du poisson et des fruits — pas de grignotines cette fois.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Des/du** — partitive and plural indefinite articles: **des** légumes (some vegetables — plural), **du** poisson (some fish — singular masculine), **des** fruits (some fruit — plural). In French, the partitive expresses 'some' before uncountable or unspecified quantities. After negation, all partitives become **de**: **pas de** légumes, **pas de** poisson — never *pas des*, *pas du*.
**Grignotines** (f.pl.) = snacks — a borrowing from Quebec French; in European French: **grignotages** or **en-cas** (snack) or **chips/biscuits apéritifs**. **Cette fois** = this time: **cette** (this — feminine to agree with **fois**). Compare: **cette fois-ci** (this time — emphatic, vs another time), **cette fois-là** (that time). **Cette fois** alone is the neutral 'this time.'

sentences.base_language: Back home, he made a simple soup with vegetables and chicken.
sentences.target_language: De retour chez lui, il a préparé une soupe simple avec des légumes et du poulet.
sentences.grammarNote:
**De retour** = back, upon returning: **de retour chez lui** (back at his place, upon returning home). **De retour** is a fixed adverbial expression — it cannot be conjugated or inflected: **de retour** à la maison (back home), **de retour** au bureau (back at the office). Compare: **en rentrant** (upon returning home — with gerundive, implies simultaneous action): **en rentrant**, il a fait la cuisine.
**Chez lui** = at his place, at home: **chez** + stressed pronoun expresses being at someone's home or place: **chez** moi (at my place), **chez** elle (at her place), **chez** le médecin (at the doctor's). **Chez** has no exact English equivalent — it captures the idea of 'in the space/world of' a person. Compare: **à la maison** (at home — more literal, the physical house).

sentences.base_language: While cooking, he called his friend and told her about the doctor's visit.
sentences.target_language: En cuisinant, il a appelé son amie et lui a raconté sa visite chez le médecin.
sentences.grammarNote:
**En + participe présent** = while doing / by doing: **en cuisinant** (while cooking). The gérondif (**en** + présent participle) expresses a simultaneous action with the same subject: **en** travaillant (while working), **en** marchant (while walking). The subjects must be identical — for different subjects, use **pendant que** + clause: **pendant qu'**il cuisinait (while he was cooking — usable when subjects differ).
**Lui a raconté** = told her (a story/account): **raconter** (to recount, to tell a story) takes an indirect object for the person told: il **lui** a raconté (he told her). Compare: **dire** (to say/tell — more general) vs **raconter** (to recount/narrate — implies telling a story or account): il **lui** a dit la vérité (he told her the truth — a fact) vs il **lui** a raconté sa journée (he told her about his day — a narrative).

sentences.base_language: His friend laughed and said: "I always told you to take better care of yourself!"
sentences.target_language: Son amie a ri et a dit : « Je t'ai toujours dit de mieux prendre soin de toi ! »
sentences.grammarNote:
**Dire de + infinitif** = to tell someone to do: je t'ai dit **de** mieux prendre soin (I told you to take better care). **Dire à quelqu'un de faire** — the indirect object marks the person told (**t'**), and **de** precedes the infinitive. Compare: **dire que** + indicatif (tell that — reporting a fact): je t'ai dit **que** tu devais te reposer (I told you that you needed to rest). The **de + inf** pattern expresses a directive, **que + indicatif** reports a statement.
**Prendre soin de soi** = to take care of oneself: **prendre soin de** + noun/pronoun is the standard French expression for 'to take care of': **prendre soin de** sa santé (take care of one's health), **prendre soin de** toi (take care of yourself). Compare: **s'occuper de** (to take care of, look after — often for tasks/people needing management): **s'occuper des** enfants (look after the children).

sentences.base_language: After eating, he felt much better — his energy was returning.
sentences.target_language: Après avoir mangé, il se sentait beaucoup mieux — son énergie revenait.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Après avoir + participe passé** = after having done: **après avoir mangé** (after having eaten / after eating). The **infinitif passé** (avoir/être + participe passé after **après**) is mandatory in French when the subject is the same — you cannot use **après** + present infinitive for past actions: *après manger* is incorrect in formal French. **Après avoir mangé** (same subject), **après qu'il eut mangé** (different subjects — literary), **une fois qu'il eut mangé** (once he had eaten).
**Revenait** — imparfait for a gradual, ongoing return: **son énergie revenait** (his energy was coming back — a progressive process). The imparfait here contrasts with the passé composé: **son énergie est revenue** would imply it came back as a completed event. The imparfait captures the gradual, ongoing nature of the recovery — very similar to the English 'was returning.'

sentences.base_language: He lay down and rested for a couple of hours.
sentences.target_language: Il s'est allongé et a reposé pendant deux heures environ.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'allonger** = to lie down, to stretch out: **il s'est allongé** (he lay down). **S'allonger** (to lengthen oneself/stretch out) is the standard verb for lying down on a surface. Compare: **se coucher** (to go to bed / lie down — more general, often implies preparing to sleep): **se coucher** tôt (go to bed early). **S'allonger** emphasises the horizontal position for rest, while **se coucher** often implies actually going to sleep.
**Environ** = approximately, about — placed after a number or quantity: deux heures **environ** (about two hours). In French, **environ** typically follows the number (unlike **à peu près** or **presque** which can come before): **environ** deux heures OR deux heures **environ** (both correct, but **environ** after the number is more common in spoken French). Compare: **à peu près** deux heures (about two hours — slightly more informal).

sentences.base_language: By evening, his mood had completely improved and he felt calm and content.
sentences.target_language: Vers le soir, son humeur s'était complètement améliorée et il se sentait calme et satisfait.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'était améliorée** — plus-que-parfait of a pronominal verb: **s'était** (imparfait of être) + **améliorée** (past participle of **s'améliorer**, agreeing with **son humeur**, feminine). Pronominal verbs always use **être** in compound tenses; the past participle agrees with the reflexive pronoun (which equals the subject in most cases): son humeur s'était amélior**ée**.
**Humeur** (f.) = mood (false friend!): **l'humeur** primarily means mood in French: **de bonne humeur** (in a good mood), **de mauvaise humeur** (in a bad mood). 'Humour' in the English sense (comedy) is also **humour** in French, but **humeur** for mood dominates. **Vers le soir** = toward/by evening: **vers** expresses approximation in time: **vers** midi (around noon), **vers** 18h (around 6 p.m.), **vers le soir** (toward evening/by evening).

sentences.base_language: He promised himself to take better care of his body from now on.
sentences.target_language: Il s'est promis de mieux prendre soin de son corps à partir de maintenant.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se promettre de + infinitif** = to promise oneself to do: **il s'est promis de** mieux prendre soin (he promised himself to take better care). **Se promettre** is the reflexive form of **promettre** — the reflexive pronoun indicates the promise is directed at oneself: je **me** suis promis de travailler plus (I promised myself to work more). Compare: **promettre à quelqu'un de** faire (promise someone to do — with an indirect object): il **lui** a promis de revenir (he promised her he would come back).
**À partir de maintenant** = from now on: **à partir de** + time = starting from: **à partir de** maintenant (from now on), **à partir de** demain (from tomorrow onwards), **à partir du** 1er janvier (from January 1st). Compare: **dorénavant** (henceforth — more formal, single word) and **désormais** (from now on — literary/formal). **À partir de maintenant** is the most natural spoken French equivalent.
